content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What is the scariest actually scary horror movie that is also good and has a good plot?

**Score:** 135
**Body:** Me and my boyfriend have been watching movies lately and every movie I get him he's just like meh it's not that great and he keeps saying he suggests better movies. I want a really good horror movie that will make him shit his pants and actually make him agree that I have good movie suggestions. Or at this point any good movie with a good plot would work preferably horror

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Thing (1982) Kurt Russell at his finest.
2. The Ring

Event Horizon

Something Wicked This Way Comes
  - Event Horizon is the answer.
3. * Hereditary
* Bring Her Back
* Talk to Me
* Terrified
* When Evil Lurks
* \[REC\]
* Grave Encounters
* Session 9
* The Descent
* Martyrs(2008)
  - Fantastic suggestions! Session 9 and Rec don't get the credit they deserve!
  - Honestly watch REC. it’s fuckign scary, gents straight to the point, good plot line and is 1hr20min 
  - Go into Martyrs blind. That’ll ruin ur night
4. "Cabin in the Woods" has some scary moments, but it also talks about conventions of horror movies in really smart ways. If he's getting tired of horror movies I highly suggest this one.
5. How about some supernatural horror movies that have very effective creepy atmospheres? ......... I always find that a well written, well made atmospheric supernatural movie is far more effective than most bloody, gory, pure horror movies. So, my suggestions:

The Changeling (1980)

The Woman in Black (the 1989 British TV production, not the one starring Daniel Radcliffe)

The Signalman (1976, British TV ghost story)

Night of the Demon (1957)

The Mothman Prophecies (2002)

All of the above are saturated with atmosphere and have little (if any) blood and gore.
6. The classic "Good Horror Movie with Good Plot" are *Alien*, *The Terminator*, and *The Thing*.

## 2. I recently watched Apocalypse Now (1979), it was a very intense movie. I would describe the film as a slow burning psychological horror about the madness of war. While very brutal, I recommend viewing it through an anti-war lens.

**Score:** 62
**Body:** I’ve been studying the Vietnam War recently due to what’s been happening with the current war and its potential to be a quagmire, someone recommended that I watch Apocalypse Now which was written and directed by Francis Ford Coppola. To sweeten the pot, Coppola is the uncle of Nicolas Cage. I decided to watch it the other night- how could I resist?

The film started off slower, but throughout the entire movie it felt like I was in a pot of water slowly warming up to a boil at the end. The film is about a black ops-trained assassin protagonist hired by the US military to take out a rogue decorated Colonel in Vietnam who went mad (Col. Kurtz, played by Marlon Brando). 

I watched the theatrical version, apparently there are a few different cuts of the film, but most have suggested that the theatrical cut is the best.

Highly recommend!

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s a must watch for anyone who cares about movies at all. 

Don’t know if you need a lens recommendation; it is anti war. Pro war people should just watch it without being told what to think. 
2. It is one of the best antiwar movies ever. War's insanity manifest in Col. Kurtz. I agree that the theatrical version is better. 
3. now go watch Hearts of Darkness, the making of :)
  - Hearts of Darkness: A Filmmaker's Apocalypse is a must watch (& re-watch), and you can find it on Amazon Prime. (I bought it at the same time that I purchased the original cut of Apocalypse Now, and the Redux version (3-box DVD set) 7 or 8 years ago). Highly recommend for fellow cinephiles.
    - “I mean, I knew he was fat. But I didn’t realize he was that fat.”

“Don’t worry, Francis. I can film him in the shadows.”
4. Did you notice that the farther they got up the river and the worse and crazier things got, the more Black US soldiers they ran into?  Like at the night battle at the bridge with The Roach?   That was on purpose. 
  - Do you know who’s in charge here?
Yeah.
5. I watched this movie on acid with a friend in college and it was one of the most impactful movie watching experience of my life.
6. Theatrical cut the best, but it's not a war movie. It's an updated version of Conrad's Heart of Darkness, Vietnam War taking the place of the Congo.
7. The first time I saw it was back in 2001 I had just moved to Vancouver when the redux version was released in theatres. I walked out after the film in a stunned haze. 

## 3. WEIRD REQUEST: Looking for movies where you start with a guy working a boring and depressive office job

**Score:** 48
**Body:** The 2 best examples I can think of on the top of my head

\- Fight Club (1999)

\- Wanted (2008)



I know this is a very odd request, but these movies give me some sort of comfort? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Office Space
  - Definitely this
2. The Matrix 
3. Joe Versus the Volcano (1990)
4. Falling Down
5. After Hours (1985) by Martin Scorsese!  
6. American Beauty, I think.
7. The Incredibles

Sorry to Bother You

Working Girl
8. Office Space
9. Stranger Than Fiction

## 4. Suggestions for a dad to watch when everyone goes to bed

**Score:** 45
**Body:** I’m a dad, so this means I don’t get control of my own tv during the day, lol. When everyone is asleep, I’ve recently taken to watching movies instead of playing video games, being on my phone, etc.

Last two movies I watched (and enjoyed) were Caught Stealing, and The Rip. I know these are pretty casual movies but I just need suggestions on easy late night watches. Thank you all in advance. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. As a middle aged man who covets his midnight films, here are some of my favorites. 


*Margin Call*

*Arcadian*

*A House of Dynamite*

*Moneyball*

*The Next Three Days*

*Saturday Night*

*A Real Pain*

*The 4:30 Movie*

*Wolfs*

*Bugonia*

*The Nice Guys*

*Rock n Rolla*

*Windfall*

2. Hell or High Water - 2016 - On Netflix.

No Country for Old Men - 2006 - On Paramount.

Enjoy!
3. Rebel Ridge
4. Guy Ritchie movies 
5. The Alien anthology just hit HBOmax. Alien Theatrical Cut, Aliens Director's Cut,  Alien³ Assembly Cut. Then stop. AVP 1 optional.
6. Lock stock and 2 smoking barrels 

Layer Cake

The Gentleman 

The Dry

Summer of 84 
7.  Sicario
8. Snatch

Lock, Stock and Two Smoking Barrels

Almost Famous

Oblivion
9. Heat
10. Dredd (2012)

## 5. High-Concept Romantic Comedies like Palm Springs

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Looking for high concept/a little wacky/out of the box romantic comedies (or even just comedies) like Palm Springs. To be clear, I'm not looking for time loop movies, I am aware of Groundhog Day. I'm interested in movies that are a little weird/crazy premise.

Isn't It Romantic would be another example (meta rom-com), or Eternity with Elisabeth Olsen (rom-com about the after life).

Thank you! :)

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Happy Accidents (2000)  
Wristcutters: A Love Story (2006)  
I'm A Cyborg But That's Ok (2006)  
Safety Not Guaranteed (2012)  
The One I Love (2014)
  - Wristcutters is a wonderful movie. Tom Waits is so awesome in it 
2. If you can separate the art from the artist: Midnight in Paris
  - Woody Allen is a terrible person, but I really love that movie.
3. Timer (2009) - everyone has a timer that gives you a countdown until you meet your soulmate
4. The Lobster 
5. Albert Brooks “Defending Your Life”
6. Her
7. Stranger Than Fiction 
8. -	Click (2006)
-	The Invention of Lying (2009)
-	Stranger Than Fiction (2006)
-	The Family Man (2000)
-	Bruce Almighty (2003)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. With all his success in Hollywood and superstar status, Djimon Hounsou still visits his village in Benin with his kids.👏What is your favorite role of his?

**Score:** 5549

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Seems like no one remember him in Gladiator...
  - I will see you again, but not yet.

[Not yet...](https://youtu.be/ghxzLw2wRis?si=RzOkRFgv0_CHYBKb)
    - Cue tears. 
  - ![gif](giphy|rI9O6UXkCjvTG)
2. It was a small role, but he was perfect as Papa Midnite  


https://preview.redd.it/n1c2bxc4amwg1.jpeg?width=485&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=a56f40da59cabb727152e26b6dd692bdb38f74d7


3. Blood Diamond
  - Dude gave his heart and soul to that part. I felt his pain in every scene.
4. He's good in everything and I think it has a lot to do with his voice and presence. I really like him in Push where he is a bad guy. 
5. [deleted]
6. It has to be Amistad for me, for real.

## 2. The Cell - A great movie that is overshadowed by its negative leads J.Lo and Vince Vaughn

**Score:** 1672
**Body:** I have to state that I’m an absolute sucker for early 2000s movies, specifically in THE year 2000. We had movies like Cast Away, Gladiator, X-Men, and (my personal fave) The Emperors New Grove. There was a specific vibe that movies from 2000 have - I’m not sure how to explain it other than, shiny(?).

Pre 9/11 movies had a way of showing violence in a heavy stylized way that I think post 9/11 has (mostly) moved on from. I’m fascinated by this and no other movie comes as close as much as The Cell. It blends elements of horror, fantasy, and cop drama to various degrees of success.

I also have to admit, I don’t think this is a good movie. It works for me in ways that I believe have worked for others, but I can’t objectively say this is good.

What does work is the art direction, set design and costume design. There are not words to explain just how weird this movie can get and how unapologetic it is about it. It always surprises me that J.Lo went through with this considering the subject matter. Regardless, the violence really goes hand in hand with the imagery and it gets major brownie points for being a major release and not caring what anyone thinks of it. Tarsem Singh (director) is truly a weirdo and uses the visual aspect of filmmaking to the best of his ability.

What also works is Vincent as the serial killer. Although the movie is very surface level as to the why’s of how he turned out, he puts in such a disturbing and honest performance that you can almost forgive the weak plot. When he is onscreen you cannot look away no matter how disturbing it is.

Unfortunately that is where the praise ends. The biggest concern are the two leads, Jennifer Lopez and Vince Vaughn. Vince is okay, but in this film he looks tired and not even remotely interested in his character. Jennifer unfortunately brings this down even further. She just does not have the acting capabilities to go against the subject matter and especially her co-star, Vincent. It’s so obvious when they are both on screen that he is running circles around her which makes it painful to watch. She has a couple moments that work, specifically when she gets out in the climax and cries on the slab. Other than that both leads are wooden and have zero chemistry together. Thankfully romance is only ever insinuated as opposed to going all in.

The supporting cast rounds it out pretty well though with Jake Weber, Dylan Baker, and Dean Norris making appearances and elevating scenes whenever they are involved.

This movies sense of style is unmatched and if you’re in the mood for something disturbing it really does go there. I didn’t bring up the plot because besides the excellent dream sequences, it’s a typical race against the clock cop drama that feels very much like by the numbers. Despite me not technically liking this movie I can’t help but have a ton of respect for what it was discussing in the year 2000. For that part alone it’s part of my blu ray collection.

Has anyone seen this recently, if so what’re your thoughts on it?

Edit: it seems like I confused a good amount of people with the way I handled the naming the two Vince’s in this movie so I apologize. For reference Vince is Vaughn and Vincent is D’Onofrio. Now please get out my DMs telling me how dumb I am lol

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. VINCENT D’ONOFRIO

![gif](giphy|ZgQQr19n0oGtbIMasl|downsized)
  - VINCENT D’ONOFRIO

![gif](giphy|DYB6Z6cTCWVe8)


    - https://i.redd.it/mjjwjhgaxjwg1.gif

I have no need to say his name again. Plus, this was the same year he was in Full Metal Jacket, so you can imagine the sheer work he put in to put on the "Private Pyle" physique, and then this.

A man of talent.
      - Holy fucking shit. I watched this movie a thousand times as a kid and did not realize that was him
        - ![gif](giphy|PUPRN0DCXmMWQ)

Usually, Vincent's more of a rounded figure kind of guy.

So Adventures in Babysitting is that rare occurrence where we got to see him actively lean down and look like he could have kicked behind. It's why it was so surprising to see him be in the role of "Thor" and "Pyle" in the same year.

Even if the projects were months apart, the amount of work he put in for getting the weight on for Full Metal Jacket, to then lean down for this brief role in Adventure in Babysitting...it was a heck of a sight to see.

The only other person that came to mind immediately was Christian Bale (The Machinist shows him going immensely thin, like skeletal). Tom Hardy is also another name that came to mind too, as he was also doing work of that sort, going between weight ranges for years from just fit to really bulked up.

Edit: Full Metal Jacket came out July 10th, and Adventures in Babysitting was July 3rd. Both movies, 1 week apart, with the same guy. So it looks like either he put on the weight, then slimmed down; or he was slimmed down then put on the weight.
2. The scene where D'onofrio is walking down the throne room steps and the train of his robe is being pulled along the walls surrounding him is so damn cool.
  - WHERE DO YOU COME FROM?
3. Fantastic movie for the reasons you listed, and poor for the same. Love the art direction and the strangeness/horror of the other world. The scene with the intestines…
4. That’s THE Nofrio, dawg. 
5. I loved it. Visually breathtaking

4. Better Man is an absolutely awesome movie! But seems like no one watched it

    - Is he a monkey of some sort?

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The Boy and the Heron (2023) and Hayao Miyazaki and the Heron (2024) - Is it fair to let external context—like a filmmaker’s life (or a supplementary documentary, in my case)—shape how I judge a film, or should a movie stand entirely on its own?

**Score:** 29
**Body:** When I first watched *The Boy and the Heron* a couple of years ago, I was neither underwhelmed nor overwhelmed – just whelmed.

Recently, while revisiting several Studio Ghibli films, I decided to watch the documentary *Hayao Miyazaki and the Heron* before returning to the film. It gave me insight into some of the real-life influences behind the film, made especially interesting when I went back to compare them to some of the interpretations I saw online ([such as in this thread](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/154bkca/thoughts_on_miyazakis_the_boy_and_the_heron/)).

However, the doc also made me question how reliable that context really is – particularly when people close to Hayao Miyazaki (like his producer Toshio Suzuki) suggest that he blurs the line between fantasy and reality not only in his work, but in his personal life as well. I found myself wondering how much I should trust these kinds of material when interpreting and/or appraising the film.

In the end, I appreciated the film more and felt a deeper emotional response, especially in relation to Miyazaki’s reflections on mortality and his relationship with Isao Takahata.

So I’m left with this question: is it valid for my opinion of a film to be shaped by that kind of external context, or should I try to judge it purely on its own terms? Am I arriving at a deeper interpretation, or simply buying into a (potentially constructed?) narrative around it?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is a really bizarre question. Fair to who?

Our experiences of all the art we consume are shaped by context - whether that is extraneous knowledge, life experience or just our mood on a given day. Is this fair? Who knows? But it is an inherent part of human existence. 

It would be really strange, bordering on insane, to try and compartmentalise all our experiences from each other in the vain hope of having some kind of "pure" reaction to a specific film or other work.

Would you try to forget everything you know about Nazi Germany prior to watching *Zone of Interest*? Should I try and erase Mozart's music from my mind to ensure I have an honest reaction to *Amadeus*? What about *The Godfather II* - should I watch that before the first film?
  - I agree with this. Enjoy a film or don't; let it's themes marinate and mature in your subconscious or don't; allow the context to enrich the narrative or don't. Unless you're a professional critic I think it's fair to judge a work as you see fit. 
    - I don't see how or why professional critics could be expected to be held to different standards to the rest of us in this regard. 

Perhaps they should have to be a little more aware of their own biases and preconceptions, and willing to flag these up if they're relevant, but that's it. One of the things I value in a critic's response to a movie is the ability to contextualise it.
  - It might seem like a strange question if you’re on the “context” side. But it’s a debate that has shaped literary studies for decades, with strong opinions on both sides. I think there are tradeoffs both ways. If you put too much stock in context, one danger is that you lose sight of the work itself. Which can shape your experience in ways that might conflict with the creator’s intentions (usually they don’t want you thinking about their life, they want you experiencing the work as a contained work of art). In addition, straying too far from the work tends to promote projection of personal feelings, judgements, etc. which, again, can get us pretty far from the work itself.
    - I can see how that debate might be important if you're involved in analysing/critiquing cinema, where it's important to be aware of and honest about your own biases, but OP was just asking about whether it's "valid" to have an opinion. 
2. But then where do we stop? Is it not external context if you understand the color blue to mean one thing and the color yellow to mean another? Films don't exist in a vacuum. If you just had a lobotomy and watched a film, you wouldn't understand a damn thing. Films, as all art, exist in relation to the real world, in relation to other pieces of art, in relation to (obviously) its creator (not always the director). I would even say that \_most\_ great films are in and of themselves auto-biographical. Do Tarkovsky's films stand on their own? I think they absolutely do. Does it enrich your experience to know about his life when watching e.g. Solyaris, Zerkalo, Offret, etc.? No doubt about it.  
  
What you are essentially asking is how well and deeply you are allowed to understand the film. You are your own limitation.
3. There is a very old rule in criticism and evaluating art that if the work doesn't speak for itself, it isn't successful.

Of course, it's tricky because extra information about process and authorial intent can recolor how you see things. But you have to be careful where that info is coming from. Is it coming from other people's reading? Is it coming from promo materials? There are even times when an artists intent might be unsuccessful and the work then has a whole meaning they never saw coming. Basically, once an artist releases their work into the world, it's for the audience to determine what it is. 
  - Yeah, but it's not a very old rule. In the 1900, just 126 years ago (out of 6000 years of history) You couldn't separate the art from the artist. Before film, records and radio there were people in front of you acting, making music, and telling a story, unless it was a book. It's very normal to expect to see a human life story next to a piece of art. 

Nowadays, it's very mixed up, but the existance of Behind the scenes, Makings of, Reviews and such, talks about the need to see the other side of the story, the person or people doing the art. 
4. Everything is shaped by context. In some sense, even individual "scenes" from a film are isolated works that become enriched via their contextual association with the rest of it (ex: you could watch some specific scenes from 2001 and they would be fine aesthetic experiences). Adding context is kind of like adding more scenes to a movie. Is the movie better with more scenes? It depends.


In my opinion, you can consider different contextual bundles around a movie as legitimately different works of art. The same movie can be experienced as:
- A movie you choose randomly, and know nothing about 
- A biography + the film (which pushes you to interpret it psychologically)
- A history documentary + the film (which pushes you to view it sociologically)
- A highlights reel on YouTube 


It's incorrect to say one of these is the *true work* imo, they're all just different works. The specific qualities of the biographies or documentaries also affect your experience. Some of them take longer to get through (ex: if you need to read a whole book to go with the movie) and might not be worth it (ex: if the movie is a more personal one, you might not benefit from adding in a history lesson). Different context bundles are more enjoyable to experience than others, but there's not a one-size-fits-all answer. 


I don't even think it's easy, as a rule, to say which parts are the text vs the context. There are paintings made by schizophrenic people that are not that great in themselves, but are very interesting as reflections of the person's internal world. Saddam Hussein, Winston Churchill, and Muammar Gaddafi wrote fiction. These are probably less interesting in themselves than they are as expressions of the people. Marcus Aurelius, it's hard to say whether he's more interesting as a historical figure or as the guy who wrote the meditations. And then you obviously have Hitler's paintings which people don't consider as artworks to be enriched by his biography. When I watch old movies, I can't immediately separate out the reactions the director *intended me* to have (ex: the director is specifically indicating this person is a misogynist) versus the ones that the director *accidentally gave me* (for example, in older movies people are accidentally misogynist a lot of the time un a way that the director did not intend). This can add depth to a viewing experience, so that it feels like you're not just watching a movie but instead a fragment of the past.


I'm not sure what sort of external criteria you would use to judge if a movie *should* stand on its own. I think many movies are better as parts of larger macro-experiences that involve things like biographies or other movies. I don't think the macro-experiences are best described as extra context, though. It's incorrect to say that one scene from a movie is "the core" and the other scenes are "the context". They mutually enrich each other. Similarly, a movie enriches the author biography just as much as the bio enriches the movie (ex: if a movie has a suicidal character and the director later kills themself).


IMO, it's very hard to give a general rule about what sorts of context are useful. It's just as hard as making a general set of rules for what makes art good.
5. When your father dies, and you watch a film about fathers dying, you will react more strongly to that film. The context that we carry into film is the context we carry into films it is unique to us and can shape and define our relationships to film in ways that others without that context cannot understand. 

Meta textual elements and outside context certainly can shape how we view films and it is fair to let it influence how you engage with a piece of media. 

## 2. My Favorite Ghibli Movie

**Score:** 24
**Body:** In my opinion, Princess Mononoke is the best Studio Ghibli movie. We follow Prince Ashitaka, who needs to break his curse, and during his journey, he gets caught in the middle of a war between humans and the forest. The movie talks about the environment and how human progress is slowly destroying nature. The concept is excellent, and there could hardly be a better premise for the message it wants to convey.

​Even though many years have passed, the action and animation still look amazing today. Some visuals feel a bit creepy, but at the same time, they are incredibly beautiful. The animation is so detailed that it’s shocking to realize it was all hand drawn. It was the most expensive movie in Japan at the time. This film even inspired James Cameron to create Avatar. Even today, it is counted among Ghibli's best works, and for many people, like me, it is their absolute favorite.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Princess Mononoke is also my favourite, a real standout. Perhaps Spirited Away might take the title of the most Ghibli Ghibli film, but what I'd give for Mononoke to be the benchmark for modern animation instead of Toy Story and Shrek. 

As point of fact, it's not 100% hand-drawn. About 5 minutes of footage, mostly the writhing corruption, is digital, though Miyazaki foight to reduce the use as much as possible.
  - I've run the numbers and it's actually Laputa that's the most Ghibli Ghibli film, as it features: a princess, a simple but delicious meal, a fried egg, impractical flying machines, steampunk, a precarious walk around a high and narrow legs, pirates, two familiar character designs, and a scary but warm hearted older woman. (For a score of 10, compared to 4 for Spirited Away.)
    - Lol Ghibli peaked early according to you. No shade, just funny. 
2. While I think Miyazaki has a few masterpieces (Spirited Away, Princess Mononoke, Nausicaä), my number one favorite is Porco Rosso; everything about that movie just feels so wonderful. I love both the Japanese audio and the English dub. The animation is perfect, the story is sweet, and something about Porco is just incredibly wholesome. I can't help but smile with a giant, stupid grin on my face.

Finally, Joe Hisaishi is firing on all cylinders with the score. I'll never stop having love for that silly pig! 
3. Princess Mononoke is Miyazaki’s best, no doubt. Really think that it is essentially perfect. The art, the music, the scope, the way it deeply explores both sides of the argument. It is a must-watch on the big screen. I watched it a bunch on VHS when I was a kid but never saw it on the big screen until I saw the IMAX re-release. When I say seeing it in IMAX was life-changing…my god. The purity and the beauty of the ending never fails to bring me to tears. There is just a timeless beauty about Princess Mononoke that is so utterly transcendent. 

Princess Kaguya imho is the best Ghibli movie overall, though. Why it’s so underrated I will never understand, it is an utter masterpiece. 
  - Princess Kaguya is criminally underrated. One the best movies in the history of cinema.
  - I feel like I need to rewatch Kaguya. I watched it with my wife, and while we both liked it, I had this 'edge of my seat' anxiety feeling wondering 'what's going to happen next, where is this going, what's the plot, what's going on, how will this end' that I realize was working against me just experiencing the movie. The end happened and I was hit with this intense feeling of air being let out of a balloon; not in a good or a bad way, just rudderless. 

I honestly don't know whether I liked it or didn't like it. I had an experience that felt outside of that spectrum. Which makes it a bit harder to motivate myself to rewatch it. But maybe with some of these compliments I'll push myself again.
    - Perhaps watching again while knowing what the ending will be will re-contextualize it a bit. The entire film is about Kaguya’s experience of the joy and pain of life before her inevitable “death” i.e. having to go back to the Moon. 

In my opinion it is a hauntingly beautiful and powerful exploration of the human condition. It was Isao Takahata’s final work before his death and I truly feel he poured his entire soul into it. Every frame is imbued with so much loving detail and beauty…the flowers, the birds, the way the water of the streams ripples against the riverstones, the wind through the grass. I saw it when it came out on the big screen and the sheer depth of artistry was absolutely breathtaking and I was already feeling teary in the beginning. 

I resonate so much with Kaguya’s themes. Feeling trapped by the strictures of life and society. The hollow feeling of existence and the incessant yearning to carve meaning out of it. Feeling as if you’ve only realized what’s important too late. I think that it’s a deeply Buddhist film. The transitory nature of life, the illusion of status and wealth and office, the importance of seeking bliss through love rather than through worldly attachments. The earth and the flesh are both cages…but what beautiful and painful cages. 

I also feel like Kaguya is the most emotionally powerful of all Ghibli films along with Grave of the Fireflies (no wonder they were directed by the same dude). Miyazaki sort of goes for subtle emotion, whereas Takahata went big. I can’t watch Kaguya without ugly crying and I’ve seen it probably 5 or 6 times with various people. The ending is absolutely and utterly devastating. In the movie theater you could hear a pin drop when the credits rolled as well as a lot of sniffling and nobody got up from their seats until the music ended. I was drowning in tears. One of the most memorable theater going experiences of my life was seeing it on the big screen, I can’t even express how much it moved me. 

It could just be that it didn’t resonate with you but I do think it rewards repeated viewings to see all of the symbolism and motifs throughout. I think it’s a masterpiece for the ages and one of my all time favorite films period, not just favorite Ghibli film.

## 3. The Illusionist (2006): the pivotal horse stall scene made absolutely no sense whatsoever and completely took me out of this movie [ spoilers]

**Score:** 5
**Body:** Spoilers for this movie

so somehow the Princess or Duchess or whatever was able to time the drug so the Prince passed out in the horse stall. Unbelievable but OK no problem I'll go with it. Then she plants evidence in the horse stall hay. Got it.

Months pass. The movie doesn't say exactly how long but it says that he disappears then reappears, purchases a theater, refurbishes the theater and starts doing a new show. Obviously all that takes a while right? months at the very least. Then after all this time the Chief Inspector goes back to the horse stall and finds the evidence in the hay. Are you telling me these people didn't change the hay in the horse stall for months? MONTHS????

My friend, that's not how horse stalls work. that is not how hay works. That is not how any of this works. It's absurd on its face and really took me out of this movie

I also wasn't really feeling the chemistry between the romantic leads. Just wasn't there for whatever reason. Paul Giamatti carried the movie from my perspective. This movie is often compared to the prestige as they were released in the same year, however the prestige is literally 100 times better movie if only because “ the twist” in the illusionist was quite obvious and I think the vast majority of the viewers guessed it while it was happening

## 5. Visceral intimacy in The Bride!

**Body:** I recently rewatched The Bride! and I already found the relationship between the Bride and Frank tragic, but this time I noticed something else.
